data.title: CoreWeave Q1 Earnings: Eye-Popping Growth, But I'm Sitting This One Out
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) delivered Q1 2025 earnings results that saw its topline deliver a large beat against analyst consensus. For investors, the appeal here is that this is a pure growth business.

And yet, for my part, I can't stop myself from thinking about Warren Buffett's quote:

The great business is the one that takes very little capital and grows a lot.

In true modern fashion, CoreWeave is doing the exact opposite. It's taking a huge amount of capital as it strives to approximately triple its revenues in 2025, and hopefully double them next year.

On top of that, its balance sheet is not exactly on steady footing, as it carries more than $7 billion of net debt.

Perhaps I'm too gun-shy. After all, I believe that there are old investors. And there are bold investors. But there are no old and bold investors.

Thus, I keep this stock at neutral.

Rapid Recap

Back in April, I said,

[...] I believe that together with the funds raised from its IPO, plus committed financing, CoreWeave will continue to exist and will plow forward.

However, successfully raising capital is one endeavor. Running a profitable business is quite another.

Since then, the stock has been rocky, selling off sharply before bouncing higher. Since I'm not in the business of timing bottoms, I don't feel like I missed out here.

CoreWeave's Near-Term Prospects

CoreWeave runs a supercharged cloud platform built specifically for artificial intelligence. Unlike traditional cloud providers that were designed for hosting websites or running business software, CoreWeave’s cloud is purpose-built to handle the massive computing power needed to train and run AI models.

The idea being that it's faster and more cost-effective for AI companies to be on CoreWeave's platform than having their own infrastructure. Basically, if you're building or running cutting-edge AI, CoreWeave gives you the hyperscaler infrastructure to do it.

As you may know, there's a lot of money chasing this space, not only industry titans like AWS (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure, but Nebius (NBIS) too.

Given this balanced background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Look Impressive Now, But What's Next?

The graphic above shows investors that this business is growing at a very rapid rate, with Q1 2025 seeing its revenues leap by 420% y/y. Impressive. Astounding. But as an investor, your immediate thought is, well, that's cool, but what's next?

And here's where things get a little more complicated. We know that the guidance for Q2 2025 points to its revenue growth rates increasing by more than 200% y/y, and that's, again, impressive.

But looking through it objectively, we can see that there are clear signs of rapid deceleration.

We are not given guidance for Q4 2025, but we are given guidance for 2025 as a whole, which is expected to be 170% y/y. But the skeptic in me says, if Q1 2025 was up 420% y/y, and Q2 2025 is expecting to see less than half this growth rate, what will happen by Q4 2025?

Ultimately, you could end up with Q4 2025 growing at approximately 140% y/y, and then, the really tough comparables start the following quarter, Q1 2026.

So, basically, the bet here is that the business can continue to grow at more than 100% y/y into 2026, while delivering more than $10 billion in revenues. That's a tough ask to buy into.

Particularly, since reporting $10 billion of revenues requires a lot of skill and scalability, which is not quite the same amount of skill as for a business that just a few months back ended 2024 with less than $2 billion in revenues.

Given this line of reasoning, let's now discuss its valuation.

CRWV Stock Valuation -- 33x Forward Non-GAAP Operating Income

As an Inflection investor, I generally prefer to back companies with a net cash position, where 5% of the company's market cap is cash. Although, I do sometimes compromise on this front.

In the case of CoreWeave, the business actually holds about $7.5 billion of net debt. To put this figure in context, in the case of CoreWeave, its market cap is actually made up of close to 25% of its market cap as net debt. This, in my opinion, is a weak balance sheet.

Now, let's get to the bullishness driving the stock.

For Q2 2025, CoreWeave guides for approximately $170 million of adjusted non-GAAP operating income, which is double the figure from last year.

On top of that, its guidance right now for 2025 as a whole points to around $830 million of non-GAAP operating income, with the tacit understanding that at least $900 million of non-GAAP operating income will be reported by the time the year is done.

So, the bull case here is that investors are paying around 33x forward non-GAAP operating income for a business that is clearly growing more than 170% y/y in 2025, and probably as much as 100% y/y the following year.

Naturally, the blemish, from my perspective, is that to deliver this sort of growth, its capex presently points to around $22 billion.

So, I find it amazing that to grow its revenues by around $5 billion this year, they have to invest 4x that capital into capex.

Risk Factors to the Upside

What could cause the stock to continue to move up rapidly? There's a lot of energy in the market right now. Investors' animal spirits are strong, particularly when it comes to investing in companies with strong revenue growth prospects.

Also, more specific to CoreWeave, large enterprises are signing huge deals, like the one with OpenAI worth up to $11.9 billion, and another $4 billion expansion with a major AI firm.

Furthermore, while a few giant customers still drive most of CoreWeave's revenue, CoreWeave’s pipeline is rapidly diversifying. In fact, their recent acquisition of Weights & Biases is also opening doors to around 1,400 more AI customers, particularly enterprises.

The Bottom Line

So, while CoreWeave's growth numbers are jaw-dropping, I just can’t get comfortable with the financial foundation beneath it all. Yes, it’s doubling non-GAAP operating income and signing massive deals, but that $7.5 billion net debt and a $22 billion capex plan to fuel $5 billion in revenue growth is tough to look past.

I get the excitement, but personally, I prefer businesses that don’t need to burn quite so much capital to grow. For now, I'm staying neutral—not bearish, just cautious. Good luck readers.

data.title: What Is The Onchain Economy?
data.description:
The onchain economy represents the next phase of digital transformation built on blockchain and automation, quietly reshaping sectors far beyond crypto.

Understanding the Next Phase of Digital Transformation

Global commerce is undergoing a significant overhaul. New digital infrastructure that is decentralized (no single entity controls it) and programmable (it can be customized and automated) is replacing outdated legacy systems. This transition is unfolding alongside broader macro shifts: rising interest in alternatives to the U.S. dollar, de-globalization, and a growing focus on domestic energy and infrastructure.

The onchain economy is emerging as the connective tissue across these trends, where financial services, computing, and monetary systems are being reimagined from the ground up.

This blog defines the onchain economy, breaks down the categories that make it up, and explores its positioning to benefit from a world increasingly influenced by Bitcoin and the digital asset ecosystem.

From Online to Onchain

The onchain economy describes economic activity enabled by blockchain-based infrastructure, where transactions, data, and assets are managed directly, transparently, and efficiently, reducing reliance on traditional middlemen and intermediaries.

Over the past 25 years, most of the global economy has digitized, but not all of it has been truly transformed.

Despite decades of innovation, much of today’s digital economy still relies on outdated systems: slow payment networks, siloed databases, and frustrating intermediaries — think the Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV)’s online portal. Systems like these often feel sluggish, clunky, and frustrating — a far cry from the seamless experience users expect today. The onchain economy is different. It’s a shift in how industries handle trust, value, and coordination — using code instead of paper, automation instead of middlemen, and transparency instead of opacity.

It’s also emerging when confidence in legacy institutions is fading. Governments are exploring alternative reserve assets. Consumers are looking for digital-first stores of value. Corporations are integrating bitcoin into their balance sheets or treasury strategies. The onchain economy is the infrastructure layer enabling this evolution

Mapping the Onchain Economy

The onchain economy isn't one industry; it's a set of connected pieces working together. Here's what it looks like in practice:

Category What It Is What It Might Include Example Company
Mining Companies that validate blockchain transactions using energy-intensive computing. Specialized mining operations, including hybrid AI/crypto compute. CleanSpark, Riot Platforms
Exchanges Platforms that allow users to buy, sell, and store digital assets. Centralized trading platforms, token swap services, crypto-native brokers. Coinbase
Holders Entities holding a material amount of digital assets as a strategic or treasury reserve. Public companies with significant Bitcoin on balance sheets. Strategy, Block
Asset Managers Firms that create and manage crypto-focused portfolios for investors. Crypto ETP sponsors, actively managed crypto strategies, fund-of-fund offerings. CoinShares
Data Centers Physical infrastructure operators supporting blockchain and AI workloads. High-performance computing centers, converted mining facilities. Equinix, Digital Realty
Energy Infrastructure Providers of power and grid services for energy-intensive technologies. Utilities, nuclear, and renewable energy firms supporting blockchain Constellation Energy, NextEra Energy
Traditional Finance (TradFi) Enablers Traditional finance companies adopting blockchain for greater speed, transparency, or cost-efficiency. Fintech apps, payment networks, digital ID PayPal, Robinhood
Semiconductors/Hardware Hardware manufacturers building chips for mining, AI, and edge computing. Designers and fabricators of ASICs, GPUs, and HPC chips. Nvidia, AMD
Consumer & Gaming Applications using tokens, NFTs, or smart contracts to deliver new digital experiences. Blockchain games, digital wallets, token-powered consumer apps. MercadoLibre, Shopify
Crypto ETPs Exchange-traded products that provide exposure to digital assets through public markets. Spot Bitcoin products, Ether trackers, diversified crypto index notes. n/a

Each category reflects a different angle on how increased Bitcoin and blockchain adoption drive real economic activity, whether it's miners scaling energy usage, holders securing treasuries, or energy and data providers capturing digital infrastructure demand. Take MercadoLibre (MELI), the Amazon of Latin America, which has integrated crypto payments to help millions gain exposure to digital assets through everyday transactions. This integration allows unbanked and underbanked people to transact securely and efficiently. Or consider CleanSpark (CLSK), a Bitcoin mining company that contributes to grid stability by managing high-load computing infrastructure, a key component of how blockchain intersects with energy strategy. CleanSpark shows how blockchain-linked businesses can deliver real-world infrastructure benefits. These examples highlight how the onchain economy is already reshaping industries. Key parts of finance, energy, and computing are being rebuilt to meet the needs of a more decentralized global landscape.

Why It Matters: Building a Better System

Bitcoin adoption is accelerating among corporations, governments, and consumers, not just as an asset, but as a monetary alternative in an increasingly fragmented world. In 2024 alone, bitcoin ETPs saw $35 billion in inflows (CoinMarketCap), while stablecoins processed over $11 trillion in value, outpacing some major card networks. This is driving demand for infrastructure that supports this adoption: miners scaling energy capacity, data centers shifting workloads, and asset managers designing new ways to access the ecosystem.

Meanwhile, trends like de-dollarization, the onshoring of energy and compute infrastructure, and the rethinking of global trade flows align with onchain investment themes. From power producers and semiconductor manufacturers to public companies holding bitcoin in treasury, the onchain economy is where monetary evolution meets real-world infrastructure.

It's not just a story of blockchain efficiency—it's a broader economic shift that reflects how the next generation of financial and industrial systems is being shaped.

The Economy Is Going Onchain

The systems that power the economy are becoming more digital, more connected, and more automated. Blockchain and related technologies are starting to reshape how money moves, data is stored, and services are delivered.

This defines the onchain economy—not just crypto but an entire stack of infrastructure, starting with everything from sovereign asset strategy to domestic energy production.

This shift is still early, but it's quietly laying the foundation for the next generation of financial and industrial systems. The economy isn’t just going digital—it’s going onchain.

Disclosures

Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC): The first decentralized cryptocurrency, enabling peer-to-peer transactions without intermediaries, secured through a proof-of-work blockchain.

Risk Considerations

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

Index performance is not representative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.

Investments in digital assets and Web3 companies are highly speculative and involve a high degree of risk. These risks include, but are not limited to: the technology is new and many of its uses may be untested; intense competition; slow adoption rates and the potential for product obsolescence; volatility and limited liquidity, including but not limited to, inability to liquidate a position; loss or destruction of key(s) to access accounts or the blockchain; reliance on digital wallets; reliance on unregulated markets and exchanges; reliance on the internet; cybersecurity risks; and the lack of regulation and the potential for new laws and regulation that may be difficult to predict. Moreover, the extent to which Web3 companies or digital assets utilize blockchain technology may vary, and it is possible that even widespread adoption of blockchain technology may not result in a material increase in the value of such companies or digital assets.

Digital asset prices are highly volatile, and the value of digital assets, and Web3 companies, can rise or fall dramatically and quickly. If their value goes down, there’s no guarantee that it will rise again. As a result, there is a significant risk of loss of your entire principal investment.

Digital assets are not generally backed or supported by any government or central bank and are not covered by FDIC or SIPC insurance. Accounts at digital asset custodians and exchanges are not protected by SPIC and are not FDIC insured. Furthermore, markets and exchanges for digital assets are not regulated with the same controls or customer protections available in traditional equity, option, futures, or foreign exchange investing.

Digital assets include, but are not limited to, cryptocurrencies, tokens, NFTs, assets stored or created using blockchain technology, and other Web3 products.

Web3 companies include but are not limited to, companies that involve the development, innovation, and/or utilization of blockchain, digital assets, or crypto technologies.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future performance.

© Van Eck Associates Corporation

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bear Market Rally Pause
data.description:
Percentile Ranks

The combination of returns for the 12.5 trading-day period and 25 trading-day period (green line in Figure #1) is at the 0.998 percentile, or higher than 99.8% of the last 20 years of data.

Figure #1

The rose colored line has only risen to the 0.275 percentile. The percentile ranks on Figure #1 look quite similar to the first bear market rally after the stock market peak at the end of 2021. The purple dots at the 100% level show the peak of a bull markets on 1/1/2022 and 2/19/25. The rise of the green line to near 100% following those peaks looks very similar.

The 3 Shorter Cycles

Figure #2

The 7.45% gain over the last 12.5 days (shown in Figure #2) is at the 0.987 percentile, a tad weaker than the day before.

Figure #3

The 13.3% return over the last 25 trading-day period pushes to the 0.996 percentile, as shown in Figure #3.

It is possible the market could continue to rally from here. Theoretically, there could be a huge further gain from 0.996 to the 1.000 percentile. However, the overwhelming probability is that the market flat lines or declines from here, likely for two or more weeks.

Figure #4

The best fit cycle for the 50 trading-day period suggests a peak will come around 7/23/25. I don’t think the bear market rally is over, but the market never goes anywhere in a straight line.

Yield Curve Risk

Figure #5

I’ve made some changes since the last time I showed the potential stock market risk indicated by the yield curve. Previously, I had estimated the lead time of the yield curve based on the lead time giving the strongest correlation for the period from 1995 through 2019. In other words, I was ignoring the pandemic in estimating the lead time at 120 weeks. Figure #5 uses a 143-week lead time, which in the lead time with the strongest correlation using all the data since 1995.

The other change is that previously I calculated the yield curve by subtracting the 1-Year T-Bond yield from the 10-Year yield. What I’ve found is that subtracting the log of the 1-Year yield from the log of the 10-Year has a stronger correlation. Of course, finding a correlation doesn’t prove there is a relationship or an actual influence, but if there is an influence, using the log differences probably identifies the risk better.

I believe the yield curve strengthens the premise we are in a bear market, but obviously, we won’t know for sure until the rally fails to make a new high, and we eventually get a price decline of more than 20%.

Obviously, I’m leaving the sell call on the S&P 500. The last article had extraordinary timing on when the bear market rally would start. We shouldn’t expect a decline to start Wednesday or whatever day this article publishes. The previous spot on timing was partly luck. Still, a decline is likely in the near future.

data.title: LEO: Perfectly Sensible If You Pay Tax But If Not, Then...
data.description:
As I like repeating these days

We can only invest in the right instruments for our purpose if we grasp how and why the instrument was created. For that creation - the structure of that creation - is what determines which problems it might solve. As we only want to invest in instruments that solve the specific and particular problem we're trying to solve, then we need to grasp both ends of this problem. Which instrument solves the problem we're trying to solve?

Bonds

As ever with investing, what follows here are not categorical statements, rather tendencies. Strong tendencies but tendencies all the same. Bonds are, in one formulation, lending money to people. As such, they're likely to produce fewer capital losses - and gains - than buying equity in something, a share or stock. The likely is true, but the absolute isn't - people we might lend money to can go bust and some of them do. Changes in interest rates in extant bonds that we might buy can produce capital gains. But, by and large, bonds are safer with capital and produce an income, not a gain.

Bonds also come in three major flavours. Treasuries are lending to the Federal government. This is not wholly risk-free - governments have gone bust before now in other countries, after all. But we all take it to be true that US Treasuries are credit risk-free - possibly on the grounds that if the Feds did go bust, then we're back to a shotgun shell economy and financial returns are not going to be high on our list of concerns.

Treasuries pay interest, we know we'll get our capital back, our risks here are interest rates and inflation but not credit.

Corporate bonds are lending to companies. This normally means a higher interest rate as it means accepting credit risks. Corporations are more likely - from observation - to go bust than the Feds. So, return and risk are always correlated in financial markets thus corproates pay more interest than the Feds.

Again with tendencies, this is not always wholly and exactly true. Back a couple of decades, IBM managed to get away with at least one bond issue with a lower coupon than the same tenor of Treasuries. As above, this is always tendencies, even if very strong ones, rather than absolutes.

The usual solution to this credit risk is that we buy a portfolio, spreading that risk across many exemplars. The easiest way of doing this is to buy into a fund which might be organised as an ETF, a mutual, whatever. But that's really what we're buying when we do, that diversification.

Municipals are lending - again, largely - to sub-national US government bodies. States, towns, water board, the Tennessee Valley Authority and on and on. Such sub-national bodies can and do go bust. Therefore, we face credit risk. So, we should again look to diversification as a way of spreading and reducing the impact of that risk. Mutual funds, ETFs, they can do that for us again. Unless we're very large-scale investors, such funds are likely to be cheaper in labour and time as well.

So, funds which specialise in municipals, why not?

BNY Mellon Strategic Municipals Inc. (NYSE:LEO)

Well, the most obvious reason why not is this:

One issue is that we seem to be facing capital risk there - we'll come to that in a moment. But the other is that yield. 3.85%? But we can get that without either credit risk or capital risk in a short-term Treasuries fund like USFR. In fact, we can get better, 4.76% the day I write this. Which seems to violate our basic contention that you can only increase income by taking more risk.

Ah, but. The income from muni bonds is free of Federal income tax (again, tendency, not absolute, but close enough). This gives us the concept of tax equivalent yield. What yield would we have to get on a fully taxable investment in order to match the yield we can gain on this tax-privileged investment?

Fairly clearly, that's going to depend upon the tax rate that you face on your next dollar of income - your marginal tax rate. A good chart of this is here:

It's possible to extend such charts to other yields, to calculate them including state taxes (munis issued instate are usually free of state income tax to state residents, something that doesn't bother us here) and so on, but that's a good guide to the idea.

The higher your marginal tax rate, the more the tax break on munis is worth to you. This seems obvious enough, but it's worth making clear.

A first conclusion

Sure, munis are great, funds are great and so on. But we'd really like to have a rough and ready idea of when the tax break on munis is worth it to us. Difficult, again, to be hard and fast. But a useful rule of thumb is that if your marginal (so, next $ of income) tax rate is under 24% then you'll probably do better in non-tax privileged investments and paying the tax. If you pay naught income tax, then this is definitely true. If you're paying the top rate, the 40% and change at the Federal level, then you should very seriously be looking at munis. Whether 24% is the exact crossing over point, well, rule of thumb.

Whether you should be in munis or not depends upon the tax rate you pay. The higher, the more, the lower, the less and if none then probably not.

LEO and taxes

We can also check what the tax-e yield is on LEO:

There are varied different ways of calculating that yield. Details that don't really concern us because every fund makes their calculations the same way: so as long as we use the same measure we can compare across funds. But do note that tax-e yield is always calculated at the tippy top of the Federal income tax system:

Based on maximum combined federal and Medicare tax of 40.80%.

Which gives us the point of a fund like LEO. For those who pay income tax - and the higher the rate paid the more this is so - a fund providing a muni yield at lower headline rates than Treasuries (and definitely than corporates) can produce higher cash in hand income.

LEO holdings

We can see what the fund invests in:

So they're in medium to long-term bonds. That's where the yield is, after all - it's a standard that lending for longer periods of time produces a high coupon, higher yield. They're also about 40% leveraged, which is entirely standard for these sorts of funds.

We can see holdings in more detail too:

The full set is available here.

Closed and open-ended

There's a major distinction between funds. In an open-ended fund, when someone buys an extra unit, then that cash flows into the fund and is invested. When someone redeems then the fund has to pay out. Of course, everyone keeps a little cash margin to deal with this. But it is possible for there to be a wave of redemptions and this can be a problem. For, the cash put aside runs out, so assets must be sold, But, obviously, the most liquid assets get sold first. This can trigger a cascade, in which investors worry about being left holding the illiquid. This is actually causing the sale of all the liquid to meet redemptions and so causing the worry.

Now, in muni bonds, well, not wholly likely. But this has happened to funds in real estate and to at least one in low liquidity and private equity. But some of the lower end of these munis will be pretty illiquid. Forced sales, at speed, will gain low prices on wide spreads.

Open-ended funds in illiquid investments are not a wholly good idea.

Closed end funds, on the other had don't face this problem. Our buying and selling is just our buying and selling to each other, not redemptions from the fund. But this then causes another potential problem, which is that the price of the fund can move away from the net asset value:

This is just one of those things it's necessary to have an opinion upon. That wandering from NAV is one thing, the risk of a redemption cascade is another. There's no one true answer. Obviously, at extremes, yes there is - second and third grade commercial property should probably not be in an open fund. Treasuries obviously can be, don't need to be in a closed one. The dividing line is more a matter of personal choice for the investor than anything else.

We, as ever, have trade-offs. Closed end funds can vary from NAV, open-ended possibly face liquidity problems. Choose and choose well.

The expenses of the fund are wholly in line with the sector:

There's $650 million in the fund, subtract leverage that's $450 million or so. So, a reasonable sized such fund.

It's managed - the point here being that they pick and choose where to invest, not simply spread themselves over the market like an index fund. They've some 250 holdings.

Why I'm wrong?

In the analysis of the fund and what it does further, I'm not in the basic analysis of why munis are valuable to some and not to others. As I'm not about to say that this is better - or worse - than other muni funds then I cannot be wrong there. My point here is not to decide among the many funds pursuing the same goal. Rather, it's to explain what the goal is, why that drives construction and therefore who the target market is.

My opinion

LEO is a perfectly sensible muni bond fund. Not hugely different from many others - a closed end fund in medium to long-term munis, a managed fund with a tax equivalent yield up around 6,5, 6.5% on the distribution.

Credit risk is managed by diversification, yield is pursued by selection, redemption cascade by being closed end.

But as at the top, to use this fund properly requires grasping why it's all been done this way. If you do not pay US Federal income tax - so, are a foreigner, or have a low income - then this fund simply is not for you. It's not designed for you, doesn't solve any of your problems.

The higher your Federal income tax rate then the more this design is for you. If you are at the top end of the marginal tax rates and are also looking for a regular and secure income then LEO's just fine - you're who it is designed for. For everyone else, not so much.

data.title: Q1 2025 U.S. Retail Preview: Tariff Fears Drive Early Consumer Spending
data.description:
By Jharonne Martis

The LSEG U.S. Retail and Restaurant Q1 earnings index, which tracks changes in the growth rate of earnings within the sector, is expected to show a 7.5% growth over last year's levels. Our metrics show that seven of 10 consumer-related industries have turned negative (Exhibit 1).

Of the 197 retailers tracked by LSEG, the Broadline Retail sector is headed for the highest earnings growth rate in the first quarter, recording a 57.1% surge over last year's level. The second-strongest sector is Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure with an 11.0% growth estimate.

At the other end of the spectrum, Textiles, Apparel & Luxury Goods has the weakest anticipated Q1 2025 estimate, with profits expected to decline by -22.6% (Exhibit 1). To date, 136 of the 197 companies in our Retail/Restaurant Index have reported their EPS results for Q1 2025, representing 69% of the index.

Exhibit 1: The LSEG Retail Earnings Growth Rate - Q1 2025

Within the Broadline Retail sector, Amazon (AMZN) recorded the strongest earnings growth rate of 62.2%. The online retailer has the biggest weighting in the group and is boosting the sector's growth rate. Still, of the seven companies in this group, only three are on track to post positive estimated earnings growth for Q1, while Etsy (ETSY), Kohl's (KSS) and Macy's (M) have the weakest estimated earnings growth rates.

The second-strongest sector is Hotels, Restaurants & Leisure. Of the 49 companies in this group, 31 are on track to post positive estimated earnings growth for Q1. DoorDash Inc. (DASH) reported one of the strongest earnings growth rate of 833.3%. Similarly, Carnival Corp. (CCL) and PENN Entertainment (PENN) already recorded robust earnings growth rates of 192.9%, and 189.5%, respectively.

In contrast, the Textiles, Apparel & Luxury Goods group is on track to post the weakest year-over-year earnings comparisons. Negative growth expectations are directly responsible for the forecast decline in the overall earnings growth rate within the group. Eleven out of 20 companies struggled to match year-ago earnings growth levels. Under Armour (UA)(UAA) already reported a 172.7% decline in earnings, while Capri Holdings is on track to report a 134.5% decline in earnings growth in the first quarter of 2025.

So far, 136 companies, or 69% of those in our Retail/Restaurant Index, have reported earnings for Q1 2025. Of this group, 64% announced earnings that exceeded analysts' expectations, while 3% matched those forecasts and the remaining 33% reported earnings that fell below analysts' predictions (Exhibit 2). The blended earnings growth estimate for Q1 2025 is 7.5%.

To date, 136 companies in the Retail/Restaurant index have reported revenue for Q1 2025. For this group, the Q1 2025 blended revenue growth estimate is 2.8%; 49% have reported revenue above analyst expectations, and 51% reported revenue below analyst expectations.

Exhibit 2: LSEG Earnings Dashboard

This week in retail

LSEG IFR Markets forecasts a challenging development for April U.S. retail sales, with overall retail sales anticipated to decline by 0.1%. The ex-autos group is expected to fare slightly better, with a 0.1% rise. However, this still reflects a sluggish start to the second quarter following March's 1.4%, when consumers rushed to buy tariff-sensitive goods ahead of anticipated price hikes.

Exhibit 3: U.S. Retail Sales - April 2025

Guidance

So far, 136 retailers have reported Q1 2025 earnings; of this group, many have cited higher prices, challenging macroeconomic conditions and a cautious consumer as contributing factors. About 83% of retailers have also discussed the impact of tariffs.

The bulk of retailers still have to report Q2 2025 results. Going into the quarter, 16 retailers issued negative preannouncements, while three issued positive EPS guidance so far (Exhibit 4). Of those retailers offering revenue guidance, 14 warned of disappointing results, while 6 said revenue might be better than previously expected.

Exhibit 4: Earnings and Revenue Guidance: Q1 2025

Amazon reported revenues of $187.8 billion for Q4 2024, making it the company with the largest revenue in Q4 2024 (Exhibit 5). That marked the first time a company has surpassed Walmart (WMT), which had held the top spot for 12 years. However, Walmart is expected to reclaim the top spot in Q1 2025, with a consensus revenue estimate of $165.9 billion, ahead of Amazon's projected results.

Below is the list of the top companies by quarterly revenue for this quarter, using consensus estimates for those that haven't reported and actuals for those that have. The table below focuses solely on the S&P 500 companies, using the current constituents.

Exhibit 5: Top Revenues for Q4 2024 and Q1 2025

Retail sales

The LSEG Same-Store Sales (SSS) index is expected to see a healthy 3.8% gain in Q1 2025 (Exhibit 6). An increase of 3.0% in SSS signals that consumer spending is healthy. Looking back one year, Q1 2024 SSS notched a gain of 2.3%.

It's very important to note that due to the pandemic, the 2020-2022 results don't offer an apples-to-apples comparison of current trends relative to previous years, as many retailers were closed due to shelter in place regulations.

Exhibit 6: LSEG Same-Store Sales Index: 2021 - Present

Consumers ramped up apparel purchases ahead of the Easter holiday, boosting performance across the sector. Aritzia (OTCPK:ATZAF) is on track to deliver the strongest same-store sales result of the quarter, with a projected increase of 15.8%. Notably, five of the top ten SSS performers this quarter come from the apparel category. Among the standouts; Citi Trends (CTRN) is expected to report SSS growth of 6.0%. Similarly, Zumiez (ZUMZ) is on track to post a 4.0% gain.

Meanwhile, Lululemon (LULU), a pandemic-era favorite, continues to perform well with projected SSS growth of 3.9%, despite facing tough year-over-year comparisons.

While apparel saw strong momentum, consumers are still feeling the impact of higher food prices, prompting continued interest in discount retailers. These value-focused stores remain resilient, maintaining business volumes despite difficult comparisons. Walmart, with its strong value proposition and loyal customer base, is expected to report a 3.8% SSS increase. Costco (COST) has already reported a better-than-expected 6.8% SSS gain, beating its 5.3% estimate.

Exhibit 7: Strongest Same-Store Sales Estimates: Q1 2025 Estimate vs. Q1 2024 Actual

On the other hand, department stores continue to struggle, remaining out of favor with consumers. Kohl's and Macy's are projected to post weak Q1 2025 same-store sales of -5.4% and -4.3%, respectively (see Exhibit 8). Other underperformers, including GameStop (GME), Destination XL (DXLG), and Haverty Furniture (HVT), remain in the bottom tier as they contend with ongoing company-specific challenges.

Exhibit 8: Weakest Same-Store Sales Estimates: Q1 2025 Estimate vs. Q1 2024 Actual

Restaurant Same-Store Sales

The LSEG Restaurant Same-Store Sales index is expected to see a 1.5% growth in SSS in Q1 2025, on top of facing last year's easy comparison of -0.1%. (Exhibit 9).

Within this industry, the Casual Dining sector is on top with a 5.5% SSS estimate, stronger than the Quick Service sector. The Quick Service sector is on track to see a -0.6% SSS.

It's important to note that, once again, the 2020-2021 results don't offer an apples-to-apples comparison over previous years, given that quarantine rules and other pandemic restrictions forced many restaurants to close. As a result, a number of restaurants didn't report SSS data during the pandemic.

Exhibit 9: LSEG Restaurant Same-Store Sales Index: 2021 - Present

In the restaurant sector, approximately 60% of the names in our SSS index are on track or have reported positive Q1 2025 results. However, it's worth noting that many are up against relatively easy comparisons from the prior year. On the weaker end, Dave & Buster's (PLAY) has one of the lowest estimates at -6.4%. Similarly, Jack in the Box (JACK) is expected to report a -3.2% decline. Meanwhile, Denny's Corporation (DENN) already reported a -3.0% comp, falling short of its 2.8% estimate.

Exhibit 10: Weakest Restaurant Same-Store Sales Estimates: Q1 2025 Estimate vs. Q1 2024 Actual

On the other hand, there were bright spots as well; Brinker International (EAT) exceeded expectations with a strong 28.2% SSS, beating its 26.5% estimate. However, Shake Shack (SHAK) and Chipotle (CMG) missed their targets, reporting comps of 0.2% and -0.4%, respectively.

Exhibit 11: Strongest Restaurant Same-Store Sales Estimates: Q1 2025 Estimate vs. Q1 2025 Actual

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Making Sense Of The Fed's Big Pause
data.description:
On Wednesday, May 7th, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) released its latest policy statement, holding the federal funds rate steady at 4.25% to 4.50%.

Alongside the usual boilerplate language, the Fed reiterated its intent to reduce securities holdings and its commitment to both sides of its dual mandate: supporting maximum employment and returning inflation to its 2 percent objective.

What stood out, however, was the Fed’s unusually direct acknowledgment of the rising economic uncertainty.

In its own words, the committee is now “attentive to the risks to both sides of its dual mandate” and believes that “the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen.” That marks one of the Fed’s clearest warnings yet about the potential onset of stagflation.

But as always, the official statement only tells part of the story. The real insights often come from the press conference. Here’s our take on some key excerpts from Chairman Jerome Powell’s remarks following the Fed’s two-day policy meeting.

The Fed Is Actively Choosing Patience

"We think our policy rate is in a good place… the appropriate thing to do is to wait and see how things evolve… we don’t think we need to be in a hurry."

Powell's stance was deliberately measured. He made it clear that the FOMC sees no pressing need to move rates in either direction right now. Even as risks evolve, the committee’s decision to hold is less about confidence in stability and more about the cost of acting prematurely.

"There’s so much uncertainty… we’re going to be watching the data… the data may move quickly or slowly, but we think we’re in a good position to let things evolve."

This is an admission that the path forward is murky. The Fed is leaning on flexibility, not a firm outlook. It's a tacit acknowledgement of the high uncertainty in the market right now, emphasizing a quick response over pre-emptive measures.

Why the Fed Won’t Move Preemptively This Time

“It’s not a situation where we can be preemptive because we actually don’t know what the right response to the data will be until we see more data.”

When asked whether the Fed might cut rates in anticipation of worsening conditions, Powell clearly pushed back. He contrasted today’s environment with 2019, when the Fed executed a series of precautionary rate cuts amid weakening growth and inflation well below target. That context allowed room to act early.

“2019 we did cut three times… you had a weakening economy and you had inflation at 1.6%. That’s a situation where you can move preemptively.”

Today, Powell noted, inflation remains above the 2% target. Forecasts from both the Fed and private sector point to renewed inflationary pressure, especially if tariffs are sustained. That puts policymakers in a bind.

“Now we have inflation running above target… and we have an expectation… that we’ll see upward pressure on inflation. Most forecasters are forecasting an increase in inflation.”

Until a clear direction emerges in the data, the Fed is unwilling to guess. The message is that preemptive easing is off the table for now. For the Fed, patience isn’t just a tactical decision. It’s the default stance when risks are two-sided and the path is still forming.

Stagflation Risk Is Explicitly on the Radar

"We may find ourselves in the challenging scenario in which our dual mandate goals are in tension… the risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen."

This was Powell’s most direct acknowledgment that the economy could be entering a phase of conflicting pressures, where responding to one problem makes the other worse. That is the classic definition of stagflation risk.

"If the large increases in tariffs that have been announced are sustained, they’re likely to generate a rise in inflation, a slowdown in economic growth, and an increase in unemployment."

Rather than treating this as an abstract possibility, Powell linked the risk to concrete policy events already in motion. The Fed isn’t predicting stagflation, but it’s preparing for the conditions that could lead to it.

Sentiment Is Cracking, But the Data Hasn’t Followed - Yet

“People are feeling stress and concern… but unemployment hasn’t gone up, job creation is fine, wages are in good shape.”

Powell acknowledged the growing anxiety across households and businesses in response to rising tariffs and economic uncertainty. But he was clear: the Fed won’t respond to sentiment alone. What matters is whether those concerns translate into measurable economic deterioration.

“We see sentiment—there are concerns that higher prices may be coming… but that shock hasn’t hit yet.”

When pressed about what would trigger action, Powell returned to the same position: the Fed needs to see confirmation in hard data before making any move. Until then, the tone of the consumer or the headlines at the port aren’t enough to shift policy.

“We’re going to be looking at not just the sentiment data, but also the real economic data… it’s not at all clear what the appropriate response for monetary policy is at this time.”

The message is consistent: anxiety is rising, but the Fed is watching results, not reactions. Until labor markets or inflation prints force their hand, the policy stance remains firmly in wait-and-see mode.

Tariffs Are Now the Central Macro Variable

"The tariff increases announced so far have been significantly larger than anticipated… if sustained, they’re likely to generate a rise in inflation… a slowdown in economic growth… and an increase in unemployment."

Powell didn’t hedge here. He clearly connected trade policy not just as a risk factor, but as a potential driver of macro deterioration. This was more direct than the typical Fedspeak.

"There’s so much uncertainty about the scale, scope, timing, and persistence of the tariffs… it’s not at all clear what the appropriate response for monetary policy is at this time."

The Fed’s ability to respond is constrained not just by the data, but by a shifting fiscal landscape it doesn’t control. Powell repeatedly emphasized that tariff outcomes are shaping the Fed’s thinking, but without clarity, monetary policy will remain on pause, even as the risks accumulate.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only, and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Certain information contained in this document constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or other comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements.

data.title: U.S. Inflation Dips To Lowest Level Since 2021 As Tariffs Loom
data.description:
The US consumer price index, the market's preferred measure of inflation, is rising at the slowest rate in more than four years, but economists believe consumers will soon see the effects of President Donald Trump's tariffs.

The US consumer price index increased 2.3% year-over-year in April, the third month of annual declines in inflation growth and the smallest increase since February 2021, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported May 13. Core consumer price inflation, which removes volatile energy and food prices, increased 2.8% year-over-year, nearly unchanged from the annual growth a month before.

The data shows a clear slowing in inflation as the rate of rising prices nears the Federal Reserve's 2% growth target, but the latest data will likely not prove to be a turning point for the domestic economy with tariffs looming, Lale Akoner, global market analyst with eToro, said in an interview. Cheaper oil and the largest decline in grocery prices since 2020 helped cool inflation in April, but housing costs remain high, services are climbing and any pricing reprieve last month was at least partly assisted by retailers clearing pre-tariff inventory, according to Akoner.

"That buffer may not last," Akoner said.

There were some modest increases in furniture and electronics prices in April, but outside of that, there was no widespread evidence that tariffs were impacting the inflation data yet, according to Thomas Simons, chief US economist at Jefferies.

"Consumers may have pulled forward demand, offering retailers some opportunity to reduce discounting, but frankly, the increases are small enough that they are hard to distinguish from month-to-month noise," Simons said in an interview.

China and the US on May 12 agreed to reduce their proposed tariffs on goods for an initial 90-day period, a trade truce that could prevent a steep spike in inflation later this year but will not prevent inflation from moving up from current levels, economists said.

Due to this truce, James Knightley, chief international economist with ING, said in an interview that he now sees year-over-year core inflation peaking at just below 4%, rather than at 4.5% to 4.7% that he had forecast before the 90-day pause. Knightley expects the consumer price index to end the year up 3.7% and eventually fall to the Fed's 2% target by the end of 2026.

Tariff wave

Under the Biden administration, the majority of imports entered the US with no or minimal duties, as the effective tariff rate was about 2.3%. The Trump administration has moved this rate to a universal 10% baseline and an overall effective or average tariff rate about five times the Biden administration's rate, Akoner with eToro said.

It remains unclear whether Trump's tariff regime will cause a temporary increase in prices or more persistent inflation, but Akoner believes the true impact of these tariffs likely will not show up in the data until June or July due to imports already in transit being priced pre-tariff, a one- to two-month lag in customs data and a two- to three-month delay for government inflation data to full reflect pass-through to prices, Akoner said.

"The tariff wave will likely hit in stages, with visible price effects starting around June or July, but expectations and market reactions are already underway," Akoner said. "If Trump's policies remain in force and expand, and especially if China retaliates, the third quarter of 2025 could see the first real inflation pressure tied to tariffs."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Killam Apartment REIT: Bargains Galore In Apartment Landlords
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

Killam Apartment REIT (KMP.UN:CA) owns and operates a portfolio of apartment buildings. As per the most recent data, the multi-resident apartment portfolio accounts for 90.5% of the net operating income [NOI] for Killam. The balance is bought in by manufactured home communities [MHCs] (4.2%) and commercial properties (5.3%). This REIT has the distinction of having the largest residential presence in Atlantic Canada, with 174 of its 217 apartment properties located in the provinces of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador and Prince Edward Island. Ontario houses 27 properties, followed by 11 in Alberta and 5 in British Columbia. Over the last few years, Killam has been increasing its presence outside Atlantic Canada. The following graphic shows the progress made by this REIT since 2018 and where it plans to be positioned in 2028.

Killam pays out 6 cents in dividends on a monthly basis and based on the current price of $17.68, it yields 4.07%.

The current yield is in the same ballpark as Minto Apartment Real Estate Investment Trust (MI.UN:CA) which yields 4.03%, and a lot higher than Boardwalk Real Estate Investment Trust (BEI.UN:CA) with its sub 2.50% payout. We covered the Q1 results of both very recently (Boardwalk, Minto). While Minto has been battered and Boardwalk has left its peers in the dust over the last five years, Killam has returned a decent 33% to its investors.

Prior Coverage

We covered Killam around the end of last year. It was trading at a 25% discount to NAV (it's even cheaper now). We concluded that piece with our price and return targets for the next two years.

It seems that is a low-risk 7%-8% annual return setup from here. The dividend itself is at 4.2% and it would be fairly unusual for the funds from operations (FFO) and NAV to grow by less than 3% from here on out. You are likely to do better than that as the REIT will likely trade at NAV at some point down the line. We bought some and are now upgrading this to a BUY with a $20 price target in two years.

Source: Killam REIT: Quality Apartment Play At A Good Discount

It has had a decent performance since then and outperformed the broader markets as well.

The first quarter results were released last week. Today, we dive into them to see if the story is playing out as we expected.

Q1-2025

Powered by the lease-up of a couple of properties, Q1-2025 ended with an overall portfolio occupancy of 97.4% compared to 96.0% at March 31, 2024. Killam achieved an average rental increase of 5.1% on its apartment portfolio, which was slightly lower than the 5.4% bump that it earned last year.

Q4-2024 saw a weighted average rental rate increase of 7.9%. So while it remained lucrative in Q1, the mark-to-market rental spreads have been going down after peaking in 2024.

Some of the variance with Q4-2024 is also due to a high number of lease renewals on January 1. These renewals at the beginning of the year have more leases with rent controls on them, so Killam typically sees higher average rental rates in the subsequent calendar quarters. Coming back to the quarter that we are reviewing today, the revenue from the total rental portfolio increased by 6.3% on a year-over-year basis.

This was despite the increase in rental incentives, driven by the two lease ups in Ontario and Calgary. The operating expenses increased by 4.8%, but one of the two drivers of the increase, utility expense, is expected to be lower for the foreseeable future with the removal of the federal carbon tax effective April 1. The net operating income [NOI] increased on a year-over-year basis by 7.2%, and the funds from operations [FFO] by 9.1%.

The total FFO pot was shared by a higher number of units during the quarter, resulting in it trickling down to a 7.7% increase, on a per-unit basis. With 18 cents in distribution, Killam paid out 64% of its FFO. This would be of relative appeal to the income investors, as opposed to a REIT like Boardwalk that retains two thirds of its FFO.

Most of Killam's debt is from mortgages on the properties. At March 31, Killam's weighted average interest rate was 3.48%, a 3 basis points increase from December 31, 2024 and a 25 basis points increase from March 31, 2024.

The REIT refinanced a mortgage during the quarter at 3.67%. If we overshoot and apply a 4.50% rate for the balance mortgages maturing in 2025 and 2026, it will increase the interest expenses by around 13%.

We do this form of stress-testing to see how vulnerable any REIT is to higher rates. The amount here is higher than the 6% impact we saw for Minto. This is because Killam is starting at a lower current rate for 2025 than Minto and more importantly, only 5% of Minto's mortgages are up for renewal in the next two years. The CMHC rate at March 31 was 3.33%, so with over 83% of the Killam's mortgages insured, our 13% increase is way more drastic than the actual numbers will likely be. Running the above scenario with a 3.75% rate instead of 4.50%, gets us to an interest expense increase of around 6–7%. Coming back to Q1, Killam was solidly placed as far as the overall debt metrics were concerned, with minor improvements in most.

Overall, the REIT delivered a solid quarter.

Verdict

Currently, all Canadian apartment REITs are trading at a discount, some more than others. Discounts for a few of them can be seen below.

On some occasions, we dive deep into the NAV estimates, and this is especially true when we disagree with the NAV estimates the REIT uses. In the above cases, we agree more or less with the REITs' internal estimates. In other words, the discount is real. So which do you buy?

REITs like Boardwalk do not have a preponderance of rent control properties, unlike say a Canadian Apartment REIT (CAR.UN:CA). Killam has a balanced mix of the two. Investors that believe the market rents have peaked would prefer CAR.UN, as they benefit from a gradual increase to market rents, leaving a lot more juice in the tank from here on out. Those that believe the housing shortage will persist, will prefer REITs like Boardwalk to avoid delayed gratification. Killam can provide the best of both worlds. We own quite a few of these REITs, including Killam. We got in at an average price of $16 for this one and think it still provides a good bargain. We rate it a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FTAI Aviation Stock: Love Or Hate It - Still A Buy
data.description:
FTAI Aviation (NASDAQ:FTAI) stock has been a Wall Street darling for some time, but since invalid concerns were raised, the stock price has failed to snap back. I addressed those concerns in a prior report and the stock price has increased 15% from those levels, but the strong momentum in the stock price did not return.

In this report, I will discuss the most recent earnings to assess whether we still see a positive backdrop for FTAI Aviation or whether it is time to look for other investment opportunities.

FTAI Aviation's EBITDA Grows More Than 40%

During the first quarter, sales grew 54% year-on-year to $502 million. Aerospace Products sales rose 93% to $365 million as the company refurbished 138 modules. Lease income increased 28.6% to $68.5 million while maintenance revenues increased 8.3% to $49.6 million and revenues from asset sales declined 51% to $18.9 million.

EBITDA rose to 7% sequentially and 64% year-on-year to $269 million, which included $162 million from the leasing business and $130.9 million from the Aerospace Products business and a negative contribution of $17.4 million from corporate and intra-company eliminations. If we look a bit deeper into the results, the sequential EBITDA growth of 7% may look somewhat less impressive. EBITDA increased by $16.6 million and included a $30 million non-recurring settlement related to leasing assets in Russia. Excluding this item, higher corporate & other eliminations offset the growth that was seen for Aerospace Products. The increase in eliminations was in part driven because FTAI Aviation is transferring lease assets to its strategic partnership with ATLAS. That caused a $7 million intra-entity elimination.

At the same time, we note that vessel sales boosted corporate results by $18.7 million in Q4 2024. If we remove the non-recurring items, but leave in the eliminated gains on sales because it is likely going to be a recurring item as FTAI offloads assets to the strategic partnership, we see that sequential EBITDA growth was only 2.3%.

So, the strong aerospace product sales growth is offset by offloading the lease assets, which lowers lease revenues from the leasing segment. What I do like is that the business will more and more become a pure MRO play from an operating perspective, but my concern is increasingly becoming that the Aerospace Products growth is going to be offset by the strategic shift of moving away from its leasing segment.

FTAI Aviation Expects No Material Impact From Tariffs

Many aerospace companies initially saw their stock prices crumble under tariff fears for aerospace parts. That fear has largely disappeared, as aerospace parts should be exempt from tariffs through USMCA. The same holds for FTAI Aviation, enabling them to reiterate their $1.1-$1.15 billion EBITDA guidance for 2025 and $1.4 billion for 2026.

The company also has facilities in Montreal and Rome that could adopt a local-for-local set up if they aren’t already working in that way, and the facility in Montreal is expected to ramp up production in the balance of the year.

So, there is a lot of good stuff going on. At the same time, I am not too sure how to feel about the asset light lease model. I covered another company, Chorus Aviation, that tried to use this model, and it did not work out for them. Likely the FTAI Aviation set up comes in a somewhat different shape as it is a partnership and not necessarily an acting manager role in the lease set up, and FTAI Aviation is likely to cater to the engine requirements for the lease assets, but we really have to see how this partnership with Apollo will actually work out apart from providing FTAI Aviation with fewer assets on the balance sheet.

Is FTAI Stock Still A Buy?

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Wall Street analysts have a strong buy rating on FTAI Aviation with a $165.59 price target. I am a bit less optimistic on the price target and believe the stock price target should be $143.66, implying 21% upside, primarily driven by higher cash burn in 2024. I expect EBITDA to grow by 22.4% annually and free cash flow to improve significantly, providing support to higher share prices. So, our price target did come down from the $167 set initially, but upside remains.

We do note that the peer group valuation provides no upside for 2025, but does provide upside for the years after. At the same time, it is also important to note that the target for 2026 closely coincides with the valuation based on the company's median EV/EBITDA, and there is no company that is quite comparable to FTAI Aviation. The peer group, therefore, provides a valuation multiple that is in line with that of lessors, but FTAI Aviation is not a pure-play lessor.

Conclusion: FTAI Aviation Still Offers Value

I believe that FTAI Aviation still offers value. The shift to an asset light model is not something that I am completely charmed by, but it does mean that the company can offload lease assets and bolster its cash position. The company will keep its leasing segment, but the leasing business will primarily be scaled through the strategic partnership.

I believe that the company’s value remains understated and believe the stock remains a buy.

data.title: Apple: Facing Services Margin Erosion In The Name Of Justice (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL), the stock has gained 18% in price.

Apple's $3 trillion market valuation is significantly supported by its App Store business. This business is a cornerstone of its Services segment, which commands an approximately 30% commission on big-business transactions at this time, with a 15% fee applying to smaller companies. But now, recent court rulings and regulatory mandates threaten to dismantle this high-margin enterprise. In the U.S., a significant ruling against Apple in its long-standing legal battle with Epic Games mandates the immediate cessation of anti-competitive practices. Concurrently, the European Union's Digital Markets Act ("DMA") compels Apple to allow third-party app stores and sideloading (allowing third-party app marketplaces to install on Apple devices). Jurisdictions like Brazil and the UK are also initiating similar actions, indicating a broader trend of regulatory convergence against Apple's market practices. The major implication of these developments is that Apple's high-margin revenue streams are under threat, potentially having significant knock-on effects on the company's valuation. This comes amid an already largely sentiment-sustained valuation which is rooted in goodwill and brand power with somewhat deteriorating growth fundamentals. That said, the company still commands a rich moat and impressive ecosystem effects, which ought not to be ignored.

Regulatory Impact Analysis

Apple's App Store significantly contributes to its Services revenue. Services generated approximately $96.17 billion in FY24, up from $85.2 billion in FY23. The App Store alone probably generated about $10 billion in commissions in FY24 from U.S. operations alone. In total, about 1/3 of Apple's Services revenue is likely from the App Store. Given this significant contribution, recent regulatory actions pose a material risk to Apple's current operational model and bull-case growth outlook.

In the U.S., the April 2025 court ruling explicitly prohibits Apple from obstructing developers' ability to direct users to external payment systems. This ruling overturns Apple's efforts to levy a new 27% commission on transactions processed externally, marking a significant legal setback. If developers broadly adopt external payments, Apple could face substantial erosion of its multi-billion-dollar U.S.-based App Store revenue stream.

In Europe, due to the DMA, Apple is now mandated to allow third-party app marketplaces and direct app sideloading on its iOS ecosystem. This fundamentally undermines the exclusivity historically central to its monetization strategy. This leads to Apple's commission rate sometimes falling to ~17%, sometimes to 10%, or other times potentially eliminated if developers fully migrate to external distribution channels. As partial migration strategies, Apple has implemented new fees, like the €0.50 annual fee per app installation surpassing one million and a 3% payment processing fee for internal transactions. But even with these additional levies, significant revenue shortfalls are inevitable.

Similar regulatory frameworks are emerging in other global markets. Brazilian regulators have mandated comparable measures to those in Europe, with substantial daily fines for non-compliance. There's also growing evidence that the UK's Competition and Markets Authority is aligning with the EU's regulatory stance. In light of this international structural attack on Apple's profitability, I believe investors have a reason to be cautious on Apple, despite its robust market sentiment factors that keep its stock price relatively secure from major fundamentally induced declines.

In fiscal terms, a moderate global reduction to a 20% commission scenario implies an immediate revenue loss of approximately $9 billion annually. Given the App Store's notably high incremental margins (estimated between 70% and 80%), this translates to roughly $6–7 billion of gross profit erosion annually, equating to a significant EPS impact of about $0.40–0.50 on Apple's earnings per share. More aggressive scenarios (like commission reductions to 15% or even 10% globally) could further exacerbate revenue losses and margin compressions, potentially reducing EPS by as much as $0.60–1.00.

I believe the most obvious general counter to this structural challenge would be to successfully integrate strong innovation within Apple's culture. Though, this is easier said than done. As it stands, Apple's walled garden is certainly under siege, and investors are holding on to a stock that is vulnerable to decline in theory, even if not in practice. I'd feel more secure holding a stock that is both theoretically/fundamentally secure and practically so.

Valuation Analysis

For the purposes of this analysis, I will consider the Services-based regulatory impact as the highest contributing factor of near-term P/E compression. Other P/E compression is perhaps most notable from the U.S. manufacturing revitalization and anti-China trade policies. However, Apple will likely be circumnavigating these depletive effects by migrating much of its supply chain to India, Vietnam, and even somewhat to the States.

In scenario one, I consider a 20% commission, which appears the most likely sustained current outcome and is my base-case supposition. This suggests revenue loss of approximately $9 billion annually, reducing net income by roughly 7%, translating to the aforementioned $0.40–0.50 GAAP EPS impact. Still, GAAP EPS will likely come in above $7.50 for FY26. Apple's valuation might contract from the present ~30 TTM figure to ~27.5. This leads to a base-case price target of ~$215, if we use a $7.65 GAAP EPS estimate and a P/E ratio of 28.

In scenario two, I consider a 15% commission, which would be the result of further mounting and compounding pressure from companies and regulatory bodies taking aim at Apple's supposed anti-competitive behavior. Revenue declines of about $13 billion annually appears likely here, with net income down by around 10%, and GAAP EPS dropping by about $0.65. Still, FY26 GAAP EPS will likely be above $7.25. As this would substantially impact the company's earnings trajectory, a further P/E contraction toward 25x or below is not unlikely.

In scenario three, I take an extreme view for the purposes of downside-risk awareness. Apple's App Store revenue could be slashed by about $18 billion on an annual basis. This would equate to about a 15–18% net income erosion and profound EPS declines by ~$1. FY26 GAAP EPS could come in below $7. The downside potential here could be stark, with severe P/E compression that the company would need to offset through AI-related internal efficiency gains, like operational leanness, and also the aforementioned revitalization of innovation. This negative outcome is low-risk, at less than a 10% possibility.

Conclusion: Hold

Apple's App Store business model is definitely facing severe regulatory siege. This requires a reevaluation of both its financial prospects and market valuation. This is undeniably true, given that anti-competitive practices in the U.S. and Europe represent not merely a temporary setback but potentially a structural reshaping of Apple's high-margin Services segment. Apple could circumvent these issues with strategic compliance wins, innovation in revenue models, or even broader strategic pivots. Such approaches will largely determine the resilience of Apple's valuation. My base-case target outlines a $215 12-month stock price, and this is very likely. That said, the downside risks are too severe and the company's innovation level is lower than I would like for a Buy rating. As such, I offer a much more conservative Hold rating. Albeit, this is an upgrade from my prior Sell rating, as I have started to acknowledge the stickiness of Apple stock's sentiment amid its strong product ecosystem, brand, and shareholder-friendly stock buybacks.
